full_response:
Let  

• frame A = rest frame of the Argonaut  
• frame B = rest frame of the Barracuda  

Choose the spatial x–axis to lie along the common line of motion and point from the
tail toward the head of the Argonaut.   
Let the moment when the two TAILS are exactly opposite one another be called
event O.  
We set the origins of the two coordinate systems at this event

 event O : (t_A ,x_A ) = (0,0) and (t_B ,x_B ) = (0,0).

1.  Motion of the Barracuda tail in frame A  
 In frame A the Barracuda moves toward the ‑x direction with speed v, so the
world-line of its tail is  

  x_tail^A(t_A)= −v t_A .                                                          (1)

2.  Event when the Barracuda tail reaches the Argonaut head (event E)

 The Argonaut is at rest in frame A with proper length L₀, so its head is fixed at  

  x_head^A = L₀ .                                                                  (2)

 Set x_tail^A = x_head^A to find the A–time of the alignment:

  −v t_A = L₀  ⇒ t_A(E) = − L₀ / v .                                            (3)

 Thus in frame A  

 event E : (t_A ,x_A ) = ( −L₀ / v ,  L₀ ).                                       (4)

3.  Transform event E to the Barracuda frame

 The Barracuda (frame B) moves with velocity +v relative to frame A, i.e. β = v/c,
γ = 1/√(1−β²).  
 For a boost with velocity −v (because B moves at −v in A) the Lorentz formulas are

  ct_B = γ (ct_A + β x_A),  x_B = γ (x_A + v t_A).                            (5)

 Insert (4):

 x_B(E)  = γ [ L₀ + v ( −L₀ / v ) ] = 0 , (as expected, it is the Barracuda tail)  
 ct_B(E) = γ [ c( −L₀ / v ) + β L₀ ]  
      = γ L₀ ( − c / v + v / c )  
      = − γ L₀ (1 − v² / c² ) / v  
      = − L₀ /(γ v).                                                            (6)

 Hence in frame B

 event E : (t_B ,x_B ) = ( − L₀ /( γ v ) , 0 ).                                 (7)

4.  Optional time–shift

 If one prefers to label the firing event itself as t = 0 in the Argonaut frame
(add the constant L₀ / v to every t_A and L₀ /(γ v) to every t_B), the spatial
coordinates are unchanged while the times become

 frame A : (t_A ,x_A) = (0 , L₀)  
 frame B : (t_B ,x_B) = ( γ β L₀ / c , 0).

Either set of coordinates describes the same physical event; the two differ only by a
trivial translation of the time origins.

\boxed{%
\begin{aligned}
\text{In Argonaut rest frame (A):}&\qquad (t_A ,x_A)=\left(-\frac{L_0}{v},\,L_0\right),\\
\text{In Barracuda rest frame (B):}&\qquad (t_B ,x_B)=\left(-\frac{L_0}{\gamma v},\,0\right),
\quad\gamma=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-v^2/c^2}}.
\end{aligned}} 

Confidence: 28%
